agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Proposal to reduce delivery record retention from 7 years to 90 days, framed as infrastructure optimization. The platform operates in EU/UK markets serving both B2C consumers and B2B logistics companies who rely on delivery data for their own compliance reporting. This is NOT an infrastructure change — it is a fundamental business rule change with legal, regulatory, financial, and contractual implications.","bounded_context":"Delivery Record Management / Data Lifecycle — intersects with Compliance, Finance, Customer Service, and Partner Integration bounded contexts.","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Delivery Record","definition":"The authoritative record of a completed delivery including proof of delivery, timestamps, recipient confirmation, chain of custody, and associated metadata. Serves as the legal evidence that a contractual obligation (delivery) was fulfilled."},{"term":"Data Retention Policy","definition":"The business and legal rules governing how long records must be preserved. Driven by regulatory requirements, contractual obligations, and dispute resolution needs — NOT by storage cost preferences."},{"term":"Proof of Delivery (PoD)","definition":"Evidence that goods were delivered to the correct recipient at the correct location and time. Required for dispute resolution, insurance claims, and regulatory audits."},{"term":"B2B Compliance Reporting","definition":"The use of delivery data by business customers to satisfy their own legal and regulatory obligations, including tax filings, customs declarations, and audit trails."},{"term":"Statutory Retention Period","definition":"The legally mandated minimum period for which commercial records must be retained, typically 6-10 years depending on jurisdiction and record type."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"Delivery records that constitute commercial transaction evidence must be retained for the minimum statutory period required by the applicable jurisdiction (typically 6-7 years in EU/UK for tax and commercial law purposes).","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a legal constraint, not a business policy. UK HMRC requires 6 years of commercial records. German tax law (AO §147) requires 10 years. French commercial code requires 10 years. Violating this makes the company non-compliant with tax law — this cannot be overridden by a VP or cost optimization initiative.","testable_assertion":"No delivery record subject to statutory retention requirements is deleted before the applicable statutory retention period has elapsed."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Delivery records supporting active or potential contractual disputes must be retained for the full limitation period (6 years in UK under Limitation Act 1980, varies by EU member state).","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Destroying evidence relevant to potential litigation within the limitation period exposes the company to adverse inference, inability to defend claims, and potential spoliation sanctions. This is a legal integrity constraint.","testable_assertion":"No delivery record is deleted while within the applicable contractual limitation period for disputes."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"B2B customer delivery data must be retained and accessible for the period specified in the B2B service agreement, which customers rely on for their own compliance reporting.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Contractual obligation to B2B customers. Deleting data they depend on for their compliance reporting constitutes a breach of contract and could cause them regulatory exposure. This is a data integrity and contractual constraint.","testable_assertion":"No B2B customer delivery record is deleted before the retention period specified in the applicable service agreement."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"Personal data within delivery records must be handled in accordance with GDPR data minimization and storage limitation principles — retained no longer than necessary for the stated purpose, but not deleted before legal retention obligations are satisfied.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"GDPR Article 5(1)(e) requires storage limitation, but Article 17(3)(b) explicitly exempts data needed for legal obligation compliance. The retention floor is set by legal obligations, not by GDPR minimization alone. Deleting too early violates legal obligations; retaining too long violates GDPR. Both directions are legally constrained.","testable_assertion":"Delivery records are retained for exactly the period required by the longest applicable legal or contractual obligation, and personal data is pseudonymized or deleted once that period expires."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"Any change to data retention policy must be reviewed and approved by Legal, Compliance, and Data Protection Officer before implementation.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Under GDPR, the Data Protection Officer must be consulted on data processing changes. Retention policy changes affect regulatory compliance. Implementing without legal review creates direct regulatory exposure.","testable_assertion":"Retention policy changes have documented sign-off from Legal, Compliance, and DPO before any code is deployed."}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"B2B customer runs annual audit at day 91 and finds their delivery records have been deleted","expected_behavior":"This must never happen. B2B customers must have access to delivery data for the full contractually agreed period. Auto-deletion at 90 days would breach service agreements."},{"scenario":"Customer files a dispute about a delivery at month 4 and the record no longer exists","expected_behavior":"The company cannot defend the claim. Consumer protection laws in the EU allow disputes well beyond 90 days. Records must exist to support dispute resolution within limitation periods."},{"scenario":"HMRC or EU tax authority requests delivery records for a transaction from 3 years ago during a tax audit","expected_behavior":"Records must be produced. Inability to produce them due to voluntary early deletion constitutes a compliance failure with potential fines and penalties."},{"scenario":"GDPR data subject access request or right-to-erasure request for a delivery record still within statutory retention period","expected_behavior":"The legal retention obligation takes precedence over the erasure request per GDPR Article 17(3)(b). Record is retained but access may be restricted. The 90-day policy would incorrectly delete records that MUST be retained."},{"scenario":"Cross-border delivery between UK and EU member state with different retention requirements","expected_behavior":"The longest applicable retention period governs. For UK-Germany deliveries, German 10-year retention requirement would apply to relevant records."},{"scenario":"Insurance claim filed 18 months after delivery for goods damaged in transit","expected_behavior":"Delivery record including chain of custody and proof of delivery must be available to support or contest the insurance claim. 90-day deletion makes this impossible."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"SEVERE NEGATIVE. (1) Tax non-compliance fines: HMRC penalties up to 100% of tax due for failure to maintain records; EU member state penalties vary but are substantial. (2) Inability to defend disputes: company must absorb all contested delivery claims it cannot disprove due to missing records. (3) B2B contract breach: potential damages claims from B2B customers whose compliance reporting is disrupted. (4) Storage cost savings are trivial compared to a single regulatory fine or lost dispute. A single HMRC audit failure could cost more than years of storage.","operational":"SEVERE NEGATIVE. (1) Compliance team loses ability to respond to regulatory inquiries. (2) Customer service loses ability to resolve delivery disputes older than 90 days. (3) B2B integration partners lose access to data they depend on. (4) Finance team loses transaction evidence needed for audit trails. (5) Legal team loses evidence needed for litigation defense.","user":"SEVERE NEGATIVE. (1) B2C consumers lose ability to reference past deliveries for disputes, returns, and warranty claims. (2) B2B customers lose compliance reporting data they depend on, potentially causing THEIR regulatory non-compliance. (3) Trust erosion — customers expect delivery platforms to maintain authoritative records.","risk":"CRITICAL. This change creates direct regulatory exposure in every EU/UK jurisdiction the platform operates in. It is not an infrastructure optimization — it is a compliance policy change being mischaracterized as a technical decision. The risk profile includes: regulatory fines, contractual liability, litigation exposure, reputational damage, and potential loss of B2B customers.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"B2B Logistics Customers","impact":"CRITICAL — Lose access to delivery data needed for their own tax, customs, and regulatory compliance reporting. May trigger their own compliance failures. Likely contractual breach."},{"group":"B2C Consumers","impact":"HIGH — Lose ability to reference delivery records for disputes, returns, warranty claims, and consumer protection proceedings within statutory limitation periods."},{"group":"Finance / Tax Team","impact":"CRITICAL — Lose commercial transaction records required for tax audits. Direct regulatory exposure with HMRC and EU tax authorities."},{"group":"Legal / Compliance Team","impact":"CRITICAL — Lose evidence needed for litigation defense. Inability to respond to regulatory inquiries. Potential spoliation exposure."},{"group":"Data Protection Officer","impact":"HIGH — Must be consulted on any retention policy change per GDPR. Change as proposed was not routed through DPO."},{"group":"Customer Service Team","impact":"HIGH — Cannot resolve delivery disputes older than 90 days. Customer satisfaction will drop; escalations will increase."},{"group":"Insurance / Claims Team","impact":"HIGH — Lose chain of custody and proof of delivery records needed to process or contest insurance claims."},{"group":"Engineering / Infrastructure Team","impact":"LOW POSITIVE — Reduced storage costs, which is the sole stated benefit. This benefit is dwarfed by the risks."}]},"confidence":{"level":90,"reasoning":"The regulatory requirements for commercial record retention in EU/UK jurisdictions are well-established and not ambiguous. HMRC 6-year requirement, EU member state requirements of 6-10 years, GDPR storage limitation balanced against legal obligation exemptions — these are clear legal constraints. The B2B contractual implications are also straightforward. High confidence that this change as proposed is not viable without violating legal obligations.","high_confidence_areas":["UK/EU statutory retention periods for commercial records are well-established at 6-10 years","GDPR Article 17(3)(b) exempts legally required retention from right-to-erasure","B2B customers relying on data for compliance reporting creates contractual retention obligations","90 days is far shorter than any applicable statutory retention period"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact retention periods vary by EU member state — specific jurisdictions the platform operates in would need to be enumerated","Specific B2B contract terms would need to be reviewed to confirm contractual retention obligations","Whether any subset of delivery data (e.g., non-commercial metadata) could safely be pruned at 90 days"],"assumptions":["Delivery records include commercial transaction evidence subject to tax record-keeping requirements","B2B service agreements include data availability commitments","The platform has not obtained legal sign-off for this change","The 7-year retention period was originally set to satisfy statutory requirements, not arbitrarily"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The proposal is framed as 'no business logic changes' but it fundamentally alters a compliance-critical business rule. Reducing retention from 7 years to 90 days is not an infrastructure optimization — it is a regulatory compliance change that violates statutory record-keeping obligations in every EU/UK jurisdiction. The framing contradicts the substance of the change.","affected_stakeholders":["B2B Logistics Customers","B2C Consumers","Finance/Tax Team","Legal/Compliance","DPO","Customer Service","Insurance/Claims"],"options":["A: Reject the 90-day auto-deletion entirely and maintain current 7-year retention","B: Implement tiered storage (hot/warm/cold) to reduce costs while maintaining full retention period — move records older than 90 days to cheaper cold storage","C: Implement data minimization — after 90 days, archive to cold storage and pseudonymize personal data fields not needed for statutory retention, while preserving commercial record integrity","D: Engage Legal and DPO to conduct a proper Data Protection Impact Assessment and determine the minimum legally compliant retention period per jurisdiction"],"recommendation":"Option D first (legal review), then likely Option C (tiered storage with pseudonymization). The storage cost problem is real and solvable WITHOUT violating retention obligations. Cold storage is orders of magnitude cheaper than hot storage. Pseudonymization satisfies GDPR minimization while preserving commercial records for statutory compliance."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Auto-deletion of delivery records at 90 days violates statutory record-keeping requirements in all EU/UK jurisdictions the platform operates in (minimum 6 years UK, up to 10 years in Germany/France).","business_rule_broken":"BR-1: Statutory retention period compliance"},{"violation":"90-day deletion destroys evidence needed for dispute resolution within contractual limitation periods (6 years UK, varies EU).","business_rule_broken":"BR-2: Litigation and dispute evidence retention"},{"violation":"Deleting B2B customer delivery data at 90 days breaches service agreements where customers rely on this data for their own compliance reporting.","business_rule_broken":"BR-3: B2B contractual data availability obligations"},{"violation":"Change was proposed without Legal, Compliance, or DPO review, bypassing mandatory governance for retention policy changes.","business_rule_broken":"BR-5: Mandatory legal and DPO review for retention changes"},{"violation":"Mischaracterizing a compliance policy change as 'infrastructure optimization with no business logic changes' creates governance risk and could lead to uninformed approval.","business_rule_broken":"BR-1, BR-2, BR-3, BR-5 — all retention-related business rules are at risk when compliance changes are disguised as infrastructure changes"}]}
agent_output_excerpt: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Proposal to reduce delivery record retention from 7 years to 90 days, framed as infrastructure optimization. The platform operates in EU/UK markets serving both B2C consumers and B2B logistics companies who rely on delivery data for their own compliance reporting. This is NOT an infrastructure change — it is a fundamental business rule change with legal, regulatory, financial, and contractual implications.","bounded_context":"Delivery Record Management / D
